texts.facebook:
0.97 ROC-AUC on test. 0.61 in production. Same model. 🚨

The clue? One single feature carried 0.61 of the model's importance — basically explaining the target on its own. That's the classic sign of data leakage, not skill.

Rule of thumb: if your offline metric looks too good, don't celebrate — investigate. Check how you split the data, what snuck into your features, and when your stats got computed.

This is 1 of 23 hands-on modules at https://ml-simulator-app-production.up.railway.app/en.html?utm_source=facebook&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=organic&utm_content=2026-07-20-lesson-a875bc
texts.linkedin:
A model scored 0.97 ROC-AUC in testing.

In production, it dropped to 0.61 — worse than the manual rules it replaced.

The investigation found the classic culprit: a single feature carried 0.61 of the model's total importance. It wasn't predicting the target — it was leaking it.

The rule of thumb every ML engineer needs: a suspiciously high offline metric is not good news. It's a symptom to investigate before deployment, not a result to ship.

Three places leakage hides:

→ The split — did the model accidentally see the future?
→ The features — is one of them a consequence of the target, not a cause?
→ The timing — were stats computed using data that wouldn't exist yet at prediction time?

This kind of post-mortem thinking is exactly what separates engineers who ship reliable models from those who get paged at 11am on a Thursday.

This is 1 of 23 hands-on modules at https://ml-simulator-app-production.up.railway.app/en.html?utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=organic&utm_content=2026-07-20-lesson-a875bc

card.headline: One feature explained 0.61? That's leakage.
lines: Check the split, the features, the timing
High score + prod flop = leakage signal
Never fit preprocessing before the split
